content.explanation:
✍️ Typography Is Most of Real-World Design

It might be surprising, but a huge percentage of what makes a website look 'professional' versus 'amateur' comes down to text styling choices — not flashy colors or animations. Good spacing, readable font sizes, and sensible font choices do more heavy lifting for a clean design than almost anything else you'll learn in this module.

📏 Understanding Font Size Units

CSS gives you several units to measure font size, and choosing the right one matters more than it might seem:

  • px (pixels) — an exact, fixed size. Predictable, but doesn't scale if a user changes their browser's default text size for accessibility reasons.
  • em — relative to the font size of the PARENT element. If the parent is 16px and you set 2em, you get 32px.
  • rem — relative to the ROOT (the very top-level html element's font size), regardless of any nested parents. Generally considered the more predictable, modern choice for font sizing in real projects.

For this course, we'll mostly use px because it's the most direct to understand as a beginner — but know that em and rem exist and are common in professional codebases for better accessibility and scalability.

🔤 Font Families and Fallbacks

Not every device has every font installed. CSS lets you list MULTIPLE fonts in order of preference — the browser tries the first, and if it's not available on that device, falls back to the next, and so on, ending with a generic category as a final safety net.
content.syntax_breakdown:
🧱 Core Text Properties

p {
  font-size: 18px;
  font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
  font-weight: bold;
  text-align: center;
  line-height: 1.6;
}
  • font-size — how big the text appears
  • font-family — which typeface to use. Notice the fallback list: Arial, sans-serif means 'try Arial first; if unavailable, use any generic sans-serif font.'
  • font-weight — text thickness/boldness. Accepts keywords like normal or bold, or numeric values like 400 (normal) through 700 (bold) for finer control
  • text-align — horizontal alignment: left, center, right, or justify
  • line-height — the vertical space between lines of text within the same paragraph. A value like 1.6 means '1.6 times the font size' — this single property has an enormous impact on readability, since cramped lines are hard to read

🔤 Font Family Categories

font-family: 'Georgia', serif;       /* serif: has small decorative strokes */
font-family: 'Arial', sans-serif;    /* sans-serif: clean, no decorative strokes */
font-family: 'Courier New', monospace; /* monospace: every character same width */

Each font family in CSS belongs to a broader category. Serif fonts (with small decorative feet on letters) feel traditional/formal — common in printed books. Sans-serif fonts (clean, no decoration) feel modern and are the most common choice for websites and apps, including this course's own interface. Monospace fonts (every letter the same width) are used for displaying code, because it keeps everything neatly aligned — you'll recognize this from this course's own code editor font.

🅰️ Other Useful Text Properties

h2 {
  text-transform: uppercase;
  text-decoration: underline;
  letter-spacing: 2px;
}

text-transform: uppercase displays text in all capitals WITHOUT you having to retype it in caps — useful because it keeps your actual HTML content properly capitalized for accessibility while only changing the VISUAL display.
content.common_mistakes:
⚠️ Common Beginner Mistakes

  • Not providing a font fallback — if your chosen font isn't installed on a visitor's device with no fallback listed, the browser falls back to its own unpredictable default.
  • Skipping line-height entirely — default line spacing is often too cramped, especially for longer paragraphs of text, hurting readability.
  • Confusing font-weight with font-size — font-weight controls thickness/boldness, not size.
  • Mixing too many different fonts on one page — generally stick to one or two font families maximum for a clean, professional look.

mini_challenge.instruction:
🎯 Challenge:

1. Add a <style> tag
2. Style the body with a font-family that includes a fallback (e.g. Arial, sans-serif)
3. Style the p with a font-size, and a line-height of at least 1.5
4. Below, add a paragraph that says: typography improves readability
5. Run and verify your code

quiz_engine.question: Why is it good practice to list a fallback font, like font-family: Arial, sans-serif;?
options: It makes the text load faster
If the first font isn't available on a visitor's device, the browser uses the fallback instead
Fallback fonts are required for every CSS rule to work
quiz_engine.explanation_feedback: Correct 🎉 Not every device has every font installed — fallbacks ensure your text still displays sensibly even if your first-choice font is missing.

takeaways: Typography choices have a massive impact on how professional a site looks
font-size controls size; font-weight controls thickness/boldness
font-family should list a fallback, ending in a generic category (serif, sans-serif, monospace)
line-height controls vertical space between lines — crucial for readability
text-transform changes VISUAL capitalization without changing the actual HTML content

cheat_sheet.font-family: Typeface, with fallbacks — e.g. Arial, sans-serif
cheat_sheet.line-height: Vertical spacing between lines — improves readability
